content.text: Showback vs Chargeback: Choosing the Right Model

Showback and chargeback are two points on a maturity spectrum for GenAI cost accountability. Both rely on the same tagged token cost ledger — the difference is what happens with the numbers once they are computed. Showback surfaces cost to teams as information; chargeback transfers it to their budget as a real financial liability.
Team budget debited at month end
Mature programmes with stable cost drivers

Most organisations start with showback, validate tag accuracy over two or three billing cycles, then transition to chargeback once teams trust the data and the allocation rules are stable. Introducing chargeback with unreliable tags generates disputes that consume more FinOps time than the programme saves.

The showback report is a SQL query against the token cost ledger, formatted and delivered to budget owners before their monthly business review. The query joins the ledger with a budget table to compute utilisation percentages and flag teams approaching their cap.

-- Monthly showback report: spend by team and feature
-- Run against BigQuery or Athena over the S3 Parquet ledger
WITH
monthly AS (
    SELECT
        team,
        feature,
        model,
        SUM(prompt_tokens)     AS total_prompt_tokens,
        SUM(completion_tokens) AS total_completion_tokens,
        SUM(cost_usd)          AS total_cost_usd,
        COUNT(*)               AS request_count
    FROM `compliance_evidence.token_cost_ledger`
    WHERE
        DATE(timestamp_utc) BETWEEN DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH)
                                AND LAST_DAY(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH)
        AND env = 'production'
    GROUP BY team, feature, model
),
budget AS (
    SELECT team, monthly_cap_usd
    FROM `finops.team_budgets`
    WHERE active = TRUE
)
SELECT
    m.team,
    m.feature,
    m.model,
    m.total_cost_usd,
    m.request_count,
    SAFE_DIVIDE(m.total_cost_usd, m.request_count) AS cost_per_request,
    SAFE_DIVIDE(
        SUM(m.total_cost_usd) OVER (PARTITION BY m.team),
        b.monthly_cap_usd
    ) AS team_budget_utilisation,
    b.monthly_cap_usd
FROM monthly m
LEFT JOIN budget b USING (team)
ORDER BY total_cost_usd DESC

Generating the SQL is the easy part; getting the numbers into a format budget owners actually read is the work. The pattern below queries BigQuery, renders a Markdown summary per team, and sends it as an email via the corporate SMTP relay.

from __future__ import annotations
        import datetime, smtplib, textwrap
        from dataclasses import dataclass
        from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart
        from email.mime.text import MIMEText
        from typing import Any

        @dataclass
        class TeamShowbackRow:
            team:                str
            total_cost_usd:      float
            monthly_cap_usd:     float
            budget_utilisation:  float    # 0.0–1.0+
            top_features:        list[tuple[str, float]]   # [(feature, cost_usd), ...]
            cost_per_request:    float
            request_count:       int

        def render_team_email(row: TeamShowbackRow, month: str) -> str:
            status = (
                "OVER BUDGET" if row.budget_utilisation > 1.0 else
                "WARNING (>80%)" if row.budget_utilisation > 0.8 else
                "On track"
            )
            top = "
".join(
                f"  - {feat}: ${cost:.2f}"
                for feat, cost in row.top_features[:5]
            )
            return textwrap.dedent(f"""
                GenAI Showback Report — {row.team} — {month}
                ============================================

                Total spend (production):  ${row.total_cost_usd:,.2f}
                Monthly budget:            ${row.monthly_cap_usd:,.2f}
                Utilisation:               {row.budget_utilisation:.0%}  [{status}]
                Requests:                  {row.request_count:,}
                Avg cost / request:        ${row.cost_per_request:.5f}

                Top features by cost:
                {top}

                Full breakdown: https://finops.internal/genai/{row.team}

                This is a showback report — no budget transfer occurs.
                Reply to dispute any line item.
            """).strip()

        def send_showback_reports(
            rows: list[TeamShowbackRow],
            smtp_host: str,
            from_addr: str,
            team_emails: dict[str, str],
            month: str,
        ) -> None:
            with smtplib.SMTP(smtp_host, 587) as smtp:
                smtp.starttls()
                for row in rows:
                    to_addr = team_emails.get(row.team)
                    if not to_addr:
                        continue
                    msg = MIMEMultipart("alternative")
                    msg["Subject"] = (
                        f"[GenAI FinOps] {row.team} showback — {month} "
                        f"({row.budget_utilisation:.0%} of budget)"
                    )
                    msg["From"] = from_addr
                    msg["To"]   = to_addr
                    body = render_team_email(row, month)
                    msg.attach(MIMEText(body, "plain"))
                    smtp.sendmail(from_addr, to_addr, msg.as_string())

Chargeback requires a step beyond SQL: a formal invoice record that finance can post to the ERP general ledger. The invoice engine reads the ledger, applies internal rates, deducts any approved credits, and produces a JSON invoice document that maps to an ERP journal entry.

from collections import defaultdict
from decimal import Decimal

INTERNAL_RATE = {
    "gpt-4o":            {"input": Decimal("0.003"),  "output": Decimal("0.012")},
    "gpt-4o-mini":       {"input": Decimal("0.0002"), "output": Decimal("0.0008")},
    "claude-sonnet-4-6": {"input": Decimal("0.004"),  "output": Decimal("0.018")},
}

def build_chargeback_invoices(
    ledger_rows: list[dict],   # rows from the cost ledger query
    cost_centres: dict[str, str],  # {team: cost_centre_code}
    approved_credits: dict[str, Decimal],  # {team: credit_usd}
    billing_period: str,
) -> list[ChargebackInvoice]:
    by_team: dict[str, list[dict]] = defaultdict(list)
    for row in ledger_rows:
        by_team[row["team"]].append(row)

    invoices = []
    for team, rows in by_team.items():
        inv = ChargebackInvoice(
            team           = team,
            cost_centre    = cost_centres.get(team, f"UNKNOWN-{team}"),
            billing_period = billing_period,
        )
        for row in rows:
            rates = INTERNAL_RATE.get(row["model"], {"input": Decimal("0"), "output": Decimal("0")})
            gross = (
                Decimal(row["prompt_tokens"])     / 1000 * rates["input"] +
                Decimal(row["completion_tokens"]) / 1000 * rates["output"]
            ).quantize(Decimal("0.0001"))
            inv.line_items.append(InvoiceLineItem(
                feature           = row["feature"],
                model             = row["model"],
                prompt_tokens     = row["prompt_tokens"],
                completion_tokens = row["completion_tokens"],
                unit_rate_input   = rates["input"],
                unit_rate_output  = rates["output"],
                gross_cost_usd    = gross,
                credit_usd        = approved_credits.get(team, Decimal("0")),
            ))
        invoices.append(inv)
    return invoices

When chargeback is real money, teams dispute charges. A common dispute is 'we didn't make that call' — usually caused by a shared service calling the LLM on behalf of multiple teams but tagging all calls to one team. The dispute workflow must provide evidence that links the invoice line item back to an individual API call.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import boto3, json

class DisputeStatus(Enum):
    OPEN     = "open"
    EVIDENCE_REQUESTED = "evidence_requested"
    RESOLVED_CREDIT    = "resolved_credit"
    RESOLVED_CONFIRMED = "resolved_confirmed"

@dataclass
class ChargebackDispute:
    dispute_id:     str
    invoice_id:     str
    team:           str
    contested_usd:  float
    reason:         str
    status:         DisputeStatus = DisputeStatus.OPEN
    evidence_keys:  list[str] = None   # S3 keys from evidence store
    resolution:     Optional[str] = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.evidence_keys is None:
            self.evidence_keys = []

class DisputeResolver:
    def __init__(self, ledger_table, evidence_bucket: str) -> None:
        self._table  = ledger_table   # DynamoDB table
        self._bucket = evidence_bucket
        self._s3     = boto3.client("s3")

    def fetch_evidence(self, invoice_id: str, team: str) -> list[dict]:
        """Return raw ledger records backing this invoice line."""
        resp = self._table.query(
            IndexName = "invoice-id-index",
            KeyConditionExpression = "invoice_id = :iid AND team = :t",
            ExpressionAttributeValues = {":iid": invoice_id, ":t": team},
        )
        return resp.get("Items", [])

    def resolve(
        self,
        dispute: ChargebackDispute,
        approve_credit: bool,
        reviewer: str,
    ) -> ChargebackDispute:
        if approve_credit:
            dispute.status     = DisputeStatus.RESOLVED_CREDIT
            dispute.resolution = (
                f"Credit approved by {reviewer}: "
                f"${dispute.contested_usd:.4f} will be applied to next invoice."
            )
        else:
            dispute.status     = DisputeStatus.RESOLVED_CONFIRMED
            dispute.resolution = (
                f"Charge confirmed by {reviewer}: "
                "evidence links cost to this team's API calls."
            )
        return dispute
Unit Economics: Cost per Request by Feature

The most actionable metric from a chargeback programme is not total spend but cost per request per feature. A feature spending \$5 000/month with 500 000 requests costs \$0.01 per request — cheap relative to its value. A feature spending \$5 000/month with 1 000 requests costs \$5.00 per request and warrants immediate engineering attention.

-- Unit economics: cost per request, by feature, last 90 days
SELECT
    feature,
    model,
    SUM(cost_usd)                                       AS total_cost_usd,
    COUNT(*)                                            AS requests,
    SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(cost_usd), COUNT(*))                AS cost_per_request,
    AVG(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)              AS avg_tokens_per_request,
    -- Week-over-week cost per request trend
    SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(cost_usd), COUNT(*))
      - SAFE_DIVIDE(
            LAG(SUM(cost_usd), 7) OVER (PARTITION BY feature ORDER BY MIN(DATE(timestamp_utc))),
            LAG(COUNT(*), 7)      OVER (PARTITION BY feature ORDER BY MIN(DATE(timestamp_utc)))
        )                                               AS wow_cost_per_request_delta
FROM `finops.token_cost_ledger`
WHERE
    env = 'production'
    AND DATE(timestamp_utc) >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 90 DAY)
GROUP BY feature, model, DATE_TRUNC(DATE(timestamp_utc), WEEK)
ORDER BY total_cost_usd DESC
Moving from showback to chargeback is an organisational change as much as a technical one. The checklist below captures the gates that should pass before activating real money transfers.

content: Tag coverage ≥ 98%: fewer than 2% of production tokens are untagged in two consecutive months.
Budget table complete: every team has an agreed monthly cap and a nominated cost-centre code.
Dispute process published: teams know how to raise a dispute and the SLA for resolution (e.g. 10 business days).
Internal rate schedule approved by finance: rates are documented, versioned, and signed off by the CFO or delegate.
ERP integration tested: a dry-run invoice has been posted to the test ERP environment and reconciled.
Showback baseline validated: teams have reviewed three months of showback data and not raised material objections.
Escalation path for budget overruns defined: who approves temporary cap increases, and in what time frame?

content.text: Showback and chargeback are accountability mechanisms that build on token cost tagging. Showback provides visibility without financial consequence — the right starting point while tag accuracy is being established. Chargeback transfers real costs to team budgets, creating strong incentives for efficiency but demanding higher accuracy and a dispute-resolution process.

content: Start with showback for the first two to three billing cycles to validate tag accuracy before real money changes hands.
Use internal fixed rates for chargeback — decouples team budgets from provider price volatility and lets the platform absorb volume discounts.
The unit-economics metric (cost per request per feature) is more actionable than total spend — it reveals expensive features regardless of request volume.
Every chargeback line item must be traceable to individual API call records in the ledger — without this, dispute resolution is impossible.
Treat the showback-to-chargeback transition as an organisational change: agree on rates, cost centres, and the dispute process before the first invoice.
